question_text: The South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation (SAARC) was formally established in 1985 at a summit held in which city, and which seven countries were its founding members?
explanation: SAARC was formally established on December 8, 1985, at the First SAARC Summit held in Dhaka, Bangladesh, where the SAARC Charter was adopted. The seven founding members were India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Nepal, Bhutan, and Maldives. The initiative was originally proposed by Bangladeshi President Ziaur Rahman in 1980 as a way to promote regional cooperation despite bilateral tensions. Afghanistan joined as the eighth member only in 2007 at the Dhaka Summit. The Secretariat was later established in Kathmandu, Nepal, in January 1987.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Kathmandu, 1985 — India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Nepal, Bhutan, Afghanistan
Dhaka, 1985 — India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Nepal, Bhutan, Maldives
Colombo, 1985 — India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Nepal, Maldives, Myanmar
New Delhi, 1985 — India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Nepal, Bhutan, Maldives, Afghanistan

elimination_hints.hint: Kathmandu is the location of the SAARC Secretariat (established January 1987), not the founding summit; Afghanistan was not a founding member — it joined only in 2007.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Kathmandu (Secretariat headquarters) with the founding summit city (Dhaka), and include Afghanistan among founders.

elimination_hints.hint: Myanmar has never been a SAARC member — it is an ASEAN member; Maldives was a founding member but the summit was held in Dhaka, not Colombo.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Myanmar (ASEAN) with a SAARC member given its geographic proximity to South Asia.

elimination_hints.hint: Afghanistan was not a founding member — it joined only at the 14th Summit in 2007; SAARC had seven founding members, not eight.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students project Afghanistan's 2007 membership backwards onto the 1985 founding.

question_text: Where is the SAARC Secretariat located, and what is its primary role in the functioning of the organisation?
explanation: The SAARC Secretariat was established in Kathmandu, Nepal, on January 16, 1987, and is headed by a Secretary-General appointed on a rotational basis among member states for a three-year term. The Secretariat's primary role is to coordinate and monitor implementation of SAARC activities, service the meetings of the association, serve as a channel of communication between SAARC and other international organisations, and prepare draft documents for summits and ministerial meetings. Unlike the European Commission, the SAARC Secretariat has no supranational powers — it cannot make binding decisions, enforce compliance, or initiate policy independently. It functions as an administrative coordinator within a strictly intergovernmental framework where all decisions require unanimous consent.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: New Delhi — functions as a supranational body with power to enforce regional trade rules on member states
Dhaka — coordinates regional policy with binding decision-making authority similar to the European Commission
Kathmandu — administrative coordinator with no supranational powers, operating within a strictly intergovernmental consensus framework
Islamabad — develops and enforces the South Asian Free Trade Area regulations across all member countries

elimination_hints.hint: The Secretariat is in Kathmandu, not New Delhi; crucially SAARC has no supranational enforcement powers — all decisions require unanimity among members.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume regional organisations have supranational authority modelled on the EU when SAARC is strictly intergovernmental.

elimination_hints.hint: Dhaka hosted the founding summit in 1985 but is not the Secretariat location; SAARC is explicitly not modelled on the European Commission — it has no binding decision-making power.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the founding summit city (Dhaka) with the Secretariat headquarters (Kathmandu) and overestimate SAARC's institutional power.

elimination_hints.hint: Islamabad has hosted SAARC summits but is not the Secretariat location; SAFTA is administered through separate mechanisms and the Secretariat has no enforcement authority.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse summit hosting cities with the permanent Secretariat location and overstate SAARC's regulatory powers.

question_text: The SAARC Charter signed in 1985 establishes a specific decision-making principle that has had major consequences for the organisation's effectiveness. What is this principle and why is it significant?
explanation: Article X of the SAARC Charter explicitly states that decisions at all levels shall be taken on the basis of unanimity, and that bilateral and contentious issues shall be excluded from the deliberations of the association. This dual principle — unanimity plus bilateral exclusion — was specifically designed to prevent India-Pakistan disputes from paralysing the organisation, but has paradoxically become the core reason SAARC cannot make meaningful progress. Any single member can block any decision by withholding consent, effectively giving Pakistan a veto over India-led initiatives and vice versa. The bilateral exclusion clause also means the core India-Pakistan dispute that shapes South Asian politics cannot even be discussed within SAARC forums, forcing issues to be addressed elsewhere or not at all.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Decisions by unanimity and exclusion of bilateral/contentious issues — effectively giving each member a veto and preventing discussion of core India-Pakistan disputes
Decisions by qualified majority voting weighted by GDP — giving India dominant influence over all policy matters
Decisions by simple majority vote — allowing smaller states to outvote India and Pakistan when aligned together
Decisions by two-thirds majority with rotating chairmanship — ensuring no single country can block regional initiatives

elimination_hints.hint: SAARC explicitly rejects weighted voting — all members are formally equal and decisions require unanimity; India's economic size gives it no formal institutional advantage within SAARC.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume regional organisations use weighted voting when SAARC deliberately adopts sovereign equality to reassure smaller neighbours.

elimination_hints.hint: SAARC does not permit majority voting — Article X requires unanimity, meaning any single member including a smaller one can block decisions; this is precisely why the organisation struggles.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume majority-rule voting is standard practice when SAARC specifically avoids it to protect sovereignty concerns.

elimination_hints.hint: Two-thirds majority would weaken the veto power of any single state, but SAARC Charter requires full unanimity — making even one dissenting member able to block decisions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse SAARC's consensus rule with more flexible majority-voting rules used by other international bodies.

question_text: The South Asian Free Trade Area (SAFTA) agreement was signed in 2004 and came into force in 2006, yet intra-SAARC trade remains remarkably low. What is the approximate share of intra-SAARC trade in members' total trade, and what explains this poor performance?
explanation: Intra-SAARC trade accounts for only about 5 percent of member countries' total trade — strikingly low compared to ASEAN (around 25 percent) and the European Union (around 60 percent). Several structural factors explain this: extensive sensitive/negative lists that exclude the most tradable products from tariff concessions (Pakistan maintains particularly large exclusion lists against India), non-tariff barriers and cumbersome customs procedures, absence of meaningful transport connectivity across contested borders, and most importantly Pakistan's refusal to grant India Most Favoured Nation status despite WTO obligations until 2012, and revoking even that limited access in 2019. The India-Pakistan bilateral freeze means two of the region's largest economies trade minimally through informal Dubai-routed channels rather than direct bilateral commerce, making SAFTA largely aspirational rather than transformative.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Around 25% — held back mainly by inadequate banking infrastructure across South Asia
Around 5% — caused by large sensitive lists, non-tariff barriers, India-Pakistan MFN deadlock, and absence of cross-border connectivity
Around 40% — limited mainly by global commodity price fluctuations rather than intra-regional policy barriers
Around 15% — mostly constrained by currency exchange problems between SAARC members

elimination_hints.hint: 25 percent is approximately the ASEAN figure, not SAARC — SAARC's actual intra-regional trade is much lower; banking infrastructure is a minor issue compared to MFN deadlock and sensitive lists.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse ASEAN's intra-regional trade success (~25%) with SAARC's performance (~5%).

elimination_hints.hint: 40 percent is much higher than any realistic SAARC figure — that level would be comparable to mature trading blocs; global commodities are not the main obstacle to SAARC trade.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students overestimate SAARC integration and attribute its weakness to external global factors rather than structural internal ones.

elimination_hints.hint: 15 percent is still well above SAARC's actual ~5 percent figure; currency convertibility is a minor issue compared to the India-Pakistan MFN deadlock and the large sensitive lists maintained by members.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students identify peripheral technical issues like currency exchange rather than recognising the India-Pakistan political deadlock as the core barrier.

question_text: The 19th SAARC Summit scheduled for November 2016 was cancelled — the first major summit cancellation in the organisation's history. What triggered the cancellation and what does it reveal about SAARC's structural weakness?
explanation: The 19th SAARC Summit was scheduled to be held in Islamabad, Pakistan, in November 2016, but was cancelled after India withdrew following the Uri terror attack on September 18, 2016, in which 19 Indian soldiers were killed by militants who had infiltrated from Pakistan-administered territory. India cited cross-border terrorism as incompatible with regional cooperation, and critically, Bangladesh, Bhutan, and Afghanistan joined India in withdrawing, making the summit impossible to hold since the charter requires all members to attend. The cancellation revealed the organisation's fundamental vulnerability — since SAARC decisions require unanimity and summits require full attendance, bilateral India-Pakistan conflict can paralyse the entire organisation. No SAARC summit has been held since, meaning the organisation has effectively been in institutional suspension for nearly a decade, with India instead channelling energy into BIMSTEC and bilateral neighbourhood diplomacy.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: India withdrew after the Uri terror attack; Bangladesh, Bhutan and Afghanistan also withdrew, making the summit impossible — no SAARC summit has been held since, revealing how bilateral conflict can paralyse the whole organisation
Pakistan cancelled the summit unilaterally due to internal political instability, and was rescheduled successfully the following year
The summit was postponed because of the COVID-19 pandemic and was later held virtually in 2020
Nepal, as the SAARC chair at the time, cancelled the summit citing concerns about India's growing dominance in the organisation

elimination_hints.hint: Pakistan did not cancel — India initiated the withdrawal, followed by three other members; the summit has never been rescheduled, not rescheduled successfully.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students reverse the direction of the cancellation, assuming Pakistan backed out when India led the withdrawal over terrorism concerns.

elimination_hints.hint: COVID-19 occurred in 2020 — four years after the 2016 cancellation; SAARC summits have not resumed even virtually despite the 2020 SAARC COVID-19 video conference which was an ad hoc meeting, not a formal summit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the 2020 ad hoc SAARC COVID video conference called by PM Modi with a formal SAARC summit.

elimination_hints.hint: Nepal was the SAARC chair at the time but did not cancel — Pakistan was the scheduled host; India was the member that withdrew first over the Uri attack, not Nepal over dominance concerns.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students misidentify the chair role and misattribute the cancellation motive to smaller-state resistance against India.

question_text: BIMSTEC (Bay of Bengal Initiative for Multi-Sectoral Technical and Economic Cooperation) has increasingly been projected by India as an alternative regional platform to SAARC. What makes BIMSTEC structurally different and why does India prefer it?
explanation: BIMSTEC was formed in 1997 and currently has seven members — Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Myanmar, Nepal, Sri Lanka, and Thailand. The critical structural difference is that BIMSTEC excludes Pakistan and Afghanistan while including two Southeast Asian states (Myanmar and Thailand), bridging South Asia and Southeast Asia across the Bay of Bengal. India prefers BIMSTEC for several reasons: Pakistan's absence removes the core obstacle that paralyses SAARC; the inclusion of Myanmar and Thailand aligns with India's Act East Policy; the Bay of Bengal frame creates a natural geographic rationale for cooperation on connectivity, trade, and maritime security; and it allows India to engage with friendly neighbours without Pakistan blocking progress. After the 2016 SAARC cancellation, India hosted BIMSTEC leaders alongside the BRICS Summit in Goa, symbolically signalling the pivot. However, BIMSTEC itself has moved slowly and remains institutionally weak compared to ASEAN.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: BIMSTEC includes China and Russia alongside South Asian states, giving it strategic weight against Pakistan-led initiatives
BIMSTEC is a purely maritime security organisation that handles only naval cooperation in the Indian Ocean, complementing SAARC's economic role
BIMSTEC excludes Pakistan and Afghanistan but includes Myanmar and Thailand — removing SAARC's India-Pakistan deadlock while aligning with India's Act East Policy across the Bay of Bengal
BIMSTEC has a supranational structure with binding majority-voting rules that SAARC lacks, making it institutionally stronger

elimination_hints.hint: BIMSTEC does not include China or Russia — its seven members are Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Myanmar, Nepal, Sri Lanka, and Thailand; it is a regional not extra-regional grouping.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse BIMSTEC with Shanghai Cooperation Organisation or other groupings that include extra-regional powers.

elimination_hints.hint: BIMSTEC is multi-sectoral (its name literally includes 'Multi-Sectoral Technical and Economic Cooperation') covering 14+ sectors including trade, connectivity, and energy — not purely naval or maritime security.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students interpret 'Bay of Bengal' in the name as implying exclusively maritime focus when BIMSTEC covers broad economic cooperation.

elimination_hints.hint: BIMSTEC is actually institutionally weaker than SAARC — it also operates on consensus, has minimal Secretariat capacity (established in Dhaka only in 2014), and has held only a handful of summits.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students overestimate BIMSTEC's institutional strength when its advantage over SAARC is primarily the absence of Pakistan, not superior institutional design.

question_text: The BBIN (Bangladesh-Bhutan-India-Nepal) initiative and its Motor Vehicles Agreement represent a significant sub-regional arrangement within the SAARC framework. What does this initiative demonstrate about the future of regional cooperation in South Asia?
explanation: The BBIN Motor Vehicles Agreement was signed in Thimphu in June 2015 between Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, and Nepal to permit free movement of passenger, personal, and cargo vehicles across their borders. It emerged after Pakistan blocked a similar SAARC-wide Motor Vehicles Agreement proposed at the 18th Kathmandu Summit in November 2014. BBIN exemplifies the 'SAARC minus Pakistan' or 'variable geometry' approach that India has increasingly adopted — forming sub-regional groups among willing members when full SAARC consensus is blocked. Although Bhutan later faced domestic political difficulties ratifying the agreement, the three other members have proceeded. This pattern reveals that meaningful South Asian cooperation is increasingly happening through sub-regional configurations that bypass Pakistan's veto rather than through full SAARC frameworks, effectively fragmenting South Asian regionalism into Pakistan-exclusive sub-groups.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: BBIN is a military alliance between four South Asian states designed to respond to China's Belt and Road Initiative in the region
BBIN is a failed initiative that was abandoned in 2015 and has no continuing significance for South Asian cooperation
BBIN is a full alternative to SAARC that has replaced it as the primary platform for regional economic cooperation in South Asia

BBIN is a sub-regional 'SAARC minus Pakistan' arrangement — the Motor Vehicles Agreement emerged after Pakistan blocked a SAARC-wide version, demonstrating the variable geometry approach to bypass India-Pakistan deadlock

elimination_hints.hint: BBIN is not a military alliance — it is specifically about connectivity and cross-border vehicle movement, an economic/connectivity initiative; it has no defence dimension and is not framed against China.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students read any India-led sub-regional initiative as strategically aimed at China rather than at practical connectivity or SAARC bypass.

elimination_hints.hint: BBIN was not abandoned in 2015 — that was the year of signing; the three non-Bhutan members have proceeded with implementation, and it continues to be cited as a sub-regional connectivity model.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students conflate Bhutan's ratification difficulties with the entire initiative's failure.

elimination_hints.hint: BBIN is not a full replacement for SAARC — it is only about motor vehicle connectivity among four states; BIMSTEC is more accurately described as a SAARC alternative, and even it has not fully replaced SAARC.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students overstate BBIN as a comprehensive substitute when it is a narrow sub-regional connectivity agreement.

question_text: India's Neighbourhood First Policy under the Modi government (2014 onwards) represents a distinct approach to South Asian engagement. What are its main features and how does it relate to SAARC's institutional decline?
explanation: Neighbourhood First was announced early in the Modi government's first term with the symbolic invitation of all SAARC leaders to the 2014 swearing-in ceremony — the first time such an invitation had been extended. Its main features include: prioritising bilateral engagement over multilateral SAARC frameworks; offering asymmetric concessions and lines of credit to smaller neighbours (including the 2014 Bangladesh Land Boundary Agreement that resolved long-standing enclave disputes); rapid infrastructure and connectivity projects; and faster visa processes for neighbours. The policy effectively substitutes bilateral India-led engagement for the institutional SAARC framework — recognising that SAARC is paralysed but the need for regional engagement remains. Critics argue it has produced uneven results (relations with Nepal, Maldives, and Sri Lanka have fluctuated) and that bilateralism cannot substitute for genuine multilateral institutions in addressing region-wide challenges like climate change, connectivity, and disaster management.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: It commits India to revive SAARC as the primary vehicle for all regional engagement, with bilateral ties subordinated to the SAARC framework
It prioritises bilateral engagement and asymmetric concessions to smaller neighbours, effectively substituting India-led bilateralism for paralysed SAARC multilateralism
It is a military doctrine focused on preventing Chinese influence in South Asia through strategic basing agreements with neighbours
It is a trade-only policy that lowers tariffs on imports from SAARC members while leaving political relations unchanged

elimination_hints.hint: Neighbourhood First actually does the opposite — it de-emphasises SAARC (which is paralysed) and prioritises bilateral engagement; no Indian government since 2016 has treated SAARC as a primary vehicle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume any regional policy must centre on regional institutions when Neighbourhood First specifically sidesteps SAARC.

elimination_hints.hint: Neighbourhood First is not primarily a military doctrine — it emphasises infrastructure, connectivity, trade, and diplomacy; basing agreements are not its main instrument though strategic concerns inform it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students reduce foreign policy to military/strategic dimensions when Neighbourhood First is primarily about economic and diplomatic engagement.

elimination_hints.hint: Neighbourhood First is much broader than trade tariffs — it includes connectivity projects, lines of credit, boundary resolution, visa liberalisation, and diplomatic engagement across all dimensions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students reduce complex foreign policy doctrines to a single instrument like tariffs when they operate across multiple dimensions simultaneously.

question_text: Comparative analysis between SAARC and ASEAN is common in regional integration literature. Despite being founded earlier (ASEAN in 1967, SAARC in 1985), SAARC has dramatically underperformed. Which of the following best captures the structural reasons for this divergence?
explanation: ASEAN's relative success and SAARC's underperformance stem from several structural differences that go beyond chronology. First, ASEAN has no dominant member — Indonesia is the largest but not overwhelmingly so — whereas India's economic and military weight (roughly 75 percent of South Asia's GDP) creates asymmetry-induced suspicion among smaller neighbours who fear Indian hegemony. Second, ASEAN lacks a structural bilateral conflict comparable to India-Pakistan that can paralyse the entire organisation. Third, ASEAN developed the 'ASEAN Way' of consensus combined with non-interference, but in a context where members had few active bilateral disputes, whereas SAARC's consensus rule combines with active bilateral hostility to produce paralysis. Fourth, ASEAN's external environment (Cold War pressures, later China's rise) created external incentives for unity, whereas South Asia's Cold War alignments were divergent (Pakistan pro-US, India non-aligned) and post-Cold War China has been a point of division (Pakistan-China alignment vs India's suspicion).
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: ASEAN has larger populations and more natural resources than SAARC, which accounts for its stronger economic integration performance
SAARC was founded later than ASEAN and simply needs more time to develop — the performance gap will naturally close within a decade

ASEAN lacks a dominant hegemon or structural bilateral conflict, while SAARC combines India-Pakistan hostility with Indian economic dominance that produces asymmetry suspicion and consensus-rule paralysis
ASEAN has a formal supranational legal system while SAARC is purely intergovernmental — this legal difference explains the full performance gap

elimination_hints.hint: SAARC actually has a larger combined population than ASEAN (~1.9 billion vs ~680 million) and significant natural resources — the performance gap is not explained by demographic or resource size.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume larger population and resources automatically translate into integration success when political and institutional factors dominate.

elimination_hints.hint: SAARC has existed for 40+ years — more than enough time to have produced results; the temporal gap argument has been refuted by SAARC's stagnation, not closure, over decades.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume regional integration is a natural temporal process when political obstacles can indefinitely block progress.

elimination_hints.hint: ASEAN is also primarily intergovernmental and does not have a strong supranational legal system — it operates on the 'ASEAN Way' of consensus like SAARC; the difference is political context, not legal structure.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students overestimate ASEAN's institutional formality when its success actually comes from political consensus and absent bilateral paralysis, not supranational law.

question_text: The SAARC Development Fund (SDF), SAARC University, and SAARC Food Bank represent institutional achievements within the organisation despite its political paralysis. What does the survival of these technical arrangements tell us about the nature of South Asian regional cooperation?
explanation: The SAARC Development Fund was established in 2010 with its Secretariat in Thimphu, Bhutan, and has funded small-scale social, economic, and infrastructure projects across member states. South Asian University was established in 2010 in New Delhi as a SAARC initiative offering post-graduate programmes to students from all member states. The SAARC Food Bank was established in 2007 to provide food security support during regional emergencies, though its actual operational effectiveness has been limited. The survival of these technical arrangements despite the 2016 summit paralysis demonstrates an important pattern: functional and technical cooperation can persist even when high-level political dialogue breaks down, creating what scholars call 'technical track' or 'functional' regionalism. This parallels the Indus Waters Treaty pattern — cooperation compartmentalised from political conflict can continue if institutional stakes are modest and direct confrontation can be avoided. However, these achievements remain marginal compared to SAARC's original transformative ambitions, functioning more as symbolic markers than substantive integration.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Functional and technical cooperation can persist even during political paralysis — these institutions demonstrate 'technical track' regionalism that compartmentalises cooperation from high-level political conflict
These institutions prove that SAARC has actually been highly successful and critiques of its underperformance are exaggerated
These institutions are entirely funded by India and prove that Indian dominance is the real driver of all SAARC activity
These institutions are ceremonial and have no functional existence — they only exist on paper and are mentioned in documents without real operations

elimination_hints.hint: The question itself frames these as 'achievements despite political paralysis' — they are modest technical bright spots, not evidence that SAARC's broader failure has been exaggerated.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students swing from 'SAARC failed' to 'SAARC succeeded' based on narrow examples rather than holding a nuanced view of modest functional success amid broad political failure.

elimination_hints.hint: SDF, SAU and Food Bank have contributions from all members — not only India; although India is the largest contributor, the institutions are formally SAARC-owned and multilateral in character.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students reduce all SAARC activity to Indian dominance when smaller states also contribute and participate in these specific institutions.

elimination_hints.hint: These institutions do function — South Asian University operates in New Delhi with thousands of students; SDF has funded real projects; Food Bank exists even if under-used; they are not purely paper institutions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students overcorrect from recognising SAARC's broad failure to dismissing every institutional achievement as fictional.

question_text:
Match the following SAARC summits in Column A with their correct location, year, or key decision in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) 1st SAARC Summit | (P) Thimphu, 2010 — theme of Climate Change adopted, decade of intra-regional connectivity declared |
| (ii) 14th SAARC Summit | (Q) Dhaka, 1985 — SAARC Charter formally adopted and organisation established |
| (iii) 16th SAARC Summit | (R) New Delhi, 2007 — Afghanistan admitted as eighth member |
| (iv) 19th SAARC Summit | (S) Islamabad, 2016 — cancelled after India withdrew following the Uri attack |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: Thimphu, 2010 — theme of Climate Change adopted, decade of intra-regional connectivity declared
Dhaka, 1985 — SAARC Charter formally adopted and organisation established
New Delhi, 2007 — Afghanistan admitted as eighth member
Islamabad, 2016 — cancelled after India withdrew following the Uri attack

explanation: The 1st SAARC Summit (i) was held in Dhaka in December 1985 where the SAARC Charter was adopted (Q). The 14th Summit (ii) was held in New Delhi in April 2007 where Afghanistan was admitted as the eighth member (R). The 16th Summit (iii) was held in Thimphu, Bhutan in April 2010 with climate change as a theme and declaring an intra-regional connectivity decade (P). The 19th Summit (iv) was scheduled for Islamabad in November 2016 but was cancelled after India withdrew following the Uri terror attack (S).
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: i-P, ii-Q, iii-R, iv-S
i-Q, ii-R, iii-P, iv-S
i-Q, ii-P, iii-R, iv-S
i-R, ii-Q, iii-P, iv-S

elimination_hints.hint: P describes Thimphu 2010 (16th Summit), not the 1st Summit; the 1st Summit was the founding Dhaka 1985 event (Q), not a climate-themed summit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the founding 1st Summit with later thematic summits that introduced new issues like climate change.

elimination_hints.hint: P describes Thimphu 2010 (the 16th Summit), not the 14th Summit; the 14th Summit in Delhi 2007 is associated with Afghanistan's admission (R).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the Thimphu climate summit (16th) with the Delhi Afghanistan-admission summit (14th).

elimination_hints.hint: R describes Delhi 2007 (14th Summit with Afghanistan admission), not the 1st Summit; the 1st Summit was the founding Dhaka 1985 event where the SAARC Charter was adopted (Q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the chronologically first summit (Dhaka 1985) with the institutionally significant Delhi summit that added Afghanistan.

question_text:
Match the following SAARC-linked institutions in Column A with their correct locations and primary functions in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) SAARC Secretariat | (P) Thimphu, Bhutan — funds regional social, economic and infrastructure projects across member states |
| (ii) SAARC Development Fund | (Q) Kathmandu, Nepal — administrative coordination of SAARC activities, headed by Secretary-General |
| (iii) South Asian University | (R) New Delhi, India — post-graduate university offering programmes to students from all SAARC member states |
| (iv) SAARC Disaster Management Centre | (S) Gandhinagar, India — capacity building, early warning and regional cooperation on disaster response |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: Thimphu, Bhutan — funds regional social, economic and infrastructure projects across member states
Kathmandu, Nepal — administrative coordination of SAARC activities, headed by Secretary-General
New Delhi, India — post-graduate university offering programmes to students from all SAARC member states
Gandhinagar, India — capacity building, early warning and regional cooperation on disaster response

explanation: The SAARC Secretariat (i) is in Kathmandu, Nepal, providing administrative coordination under the Secretary-General (Q). The SAARC Development Fund (ii) is headquartered in Thimphu, Bhutan, and funds regional development projects (P). South Asian University (iii) is in New Delhi, India, offering post-graduate programmes to SAARC country students (R). The SAARC Disaster Management Centre (iv), after restructuring in 2016, is hosted in Gandhinagar, India, focusing on disaster response cooperation (S).
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: i-Q, ii-P, iii-R, iv-S
i-P, ii-Q, iii-R, iv-S
i-Q, ii-R, iii-P, iv-S
i-R, ii-P, iii-Q, iv-S

elimination_hints.hint: P describes the SAARC Development Fund (Thimphu), not the Secretariat; the Secretariat is in Kathmandu (Q), not Thimphu which hosts the Development Fund.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the Secretariat (administrative HQ in Kathmandu) with the Development Fund (in Thimphu).

elimination_hints.hint: R describes South Asian University in Delhi, not the SAARC Development Fund; the Development Fund is based in Thimphu (P), not Delhi which hosts SAU.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the Delhi-based South Asian University with the Thimphu-based Development Fund.

elimination_hints.hint: R describes South Asian University in Delhi, not the Secretariat; the Secretariat is in Kathmandu (Q) — the core SAARC administrative headquarters.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the Kathmandu Secretariat with Delhi-based SAU because India's profile makes Delhi seem like a natural HQ.

question_text:
Match the following analytical concepts used to explain SAARC's underperformance in Column A with their correct descriptions in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) Asymmetric regionalism | (P) Sub-regional arrangements like BBIN that proceed without all SAARC members — cooperation with willing participants while excluding veto-wielding members |
| (ii) Variable geometry | (Q) Structural dominance of one member (India) that produces suspicion among smaller neighbours and complicates equal-footing cooperation |
| (iii) Hostage regionalism | (R) Entire regional organisation can be paralysed by bilateral conflict between two members — SAARC held hostage to India-Pakistan relations |
| (iv) Functional spillover | (S) Liberal theory that economic cooperation in one sector generates demand for cooperation in other sectors — largely failed to materialise in SAARC |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: Sub-regional arrangements like BBIN that proceed without all SAARC members — cooperation with willing participants while excluding veto-wielding members
Structural dominance of one member (India) that produces suspicion among smaller neighbours and complicates equal-footing cooperation
Entire regional organisation can be paralysed by bilateral conflict between two members — SAARC held hostage to India-Pakistan relations
Liberal theory that economic cooperation in one sector generates demand for cooperation in other sectors — largely failed to materialise in SAARC

explanation: Asymmetric regionalism (i) refers to the structural problem of Indian dominance in South Asia creating suspicion among smaller states (Q). Variable geometry (ii) describes sub-regional arrangements like BBIN that proceed with willing members while bypassing vetoes (P). Hostage regionalism (iii) captures how bilateral India-Pakistan conflict paralyses the entire multilateral framework (R). Functional spillover (iv) is the liberal neo-functionalist theory expecting sectoral cooperation to generate further integration, which has largely failed in SAARC (S).
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: i-P, ii-Q, iii-R, iv-S
i-R, ii-P, iii-Q, iv-S
i-Q, ii-P, iii-R, iv-S
i-Q, ii-R, iii-P, iv-S

elimination_hints.hint: P describes variable geometry (sub-regional willing-members cooperation), not asymmetric regionalism; asymmetric regionalism refers to structural size imbalances (Q), not sub-regional arrangements.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse asymmetric regionalism (about power imbalance) with variable geometry (about flexible sub-groupings).

elimination_hints.hint: R describes hostage regionalism (bilateral conflict paralysing multilateral framework), not asymmetric regionalism; asymmetric regionalism is about structural dominance (Q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the paralysis effect (hostage regionalism) with the dominance effect (asymmetric regionalism).

elimination_hints.hint: R describes hostage regionalism (bilateral hostage effect), not variable geometry; variable geometry is about sub-regional cooperation patterns like BBIN (P).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the hostage effect of India-Pakistan conflict with variable geometry which is the institutional response to that paralysis.

assertion: The SAARC Charter's decision by unanimity rule and exclusion of bilateral/contentious issues, originally designed to protect the organisation from India-Pakistan conflict, have paradoxically become the core structural reasons for its institutional paralysis.
reason: Because any single member can veto decisions and core bilateral disputes cannot even be discussed, Pakistan has been able to block India-led initiatives while the fundamental India-Pakistan dispute that drives South Asian politics has remained permanently outside SAARC's agenda — making the organisation unable to address the central issues of the region.
question_text:
Assertion (A): The SAARC Charter's decision by unanimity rule and exclusion of bilateral/contentious issues, originally designed to protect the organisation from India-Pakistan conflict, have paradoxically become the core structural reasons for its institutional paralysis.
Reason (R): Because any single member can veto decisions and core bilateral disputes cannot even be discussed, Pakistan has been able to block India-led initiatives while the fundamental India-Pakistan dispute that drives South Asian politics has remained permanently outside SAARC's agenda — making the organisation unable to address the central issues of the region.
explanation: Both A and R are analytically accurate and causally connected. R identifies the specific institutional mechanisms — unanimous veto power plus bilateral exclusion — that directly explain A's paradox claim about protective rules becoming paralysing ones. The Charter framers intended to safeguard SAARC from bilateral disputes by excluding them, but this same exclusion combined with veto power means the organisation cannot discuss or resolve the conflicts that structurally shape its member relationships, producing exactly the paralysis the rules were meant to prevent.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R identifies precisely the veto-plus-exclusion mechanism that A claims produces the paradox — these are not parallel observations but explanation and explanandum of the same institutional failure.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students see A's paradox and R's mechanism as independent observations rather than as the causal link explaining the paradox.

elimination_hints.hint: R is documented in SAARC Charter Article X itself which codifies unanimity and bilateral exclusion; Pakistan has in fact repeatedly blocked India-led SAARC initiatives (Motor Vehicles Agreement, connectivity).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students question the factual basis of R when both the charter text and historical record directly support it.

elimination_hints.hint: A is supported by decades of SAARC underperformance — the paradox of protective rules becoming paralysing ones is a standard finding in South Asian regional integration literature.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students dismiss A's claim as speculative when it reflects a well-documented institutional pattern.

assertion: India's growing preference for BIMSTEC, BBIN and bilateral Neighbourhood First engagement over SAARC reflects a pragmatic abandonment of multilateral South Asian regionalism rather than a tactical pause — with no realistic pathway for SAARC revival under current conditions.
reason: The structural causes of SAARC's paralysis — the India-Pakistan conflict, the asymmetric power distribution, and the consensus-plus-exclusion charter rules — are unlikely to change in the foreseeable future, and India has concluded that investing political capital in SAARC revival produces lower returns than alternative platforms that exclude Pakistan's veto.
question_text:
Assertion (A): India's growing preference for BIMSTEC, BBIN and bilateral Neighbourhood First engagement over SAARC reflects a pragmatic abandonment of multilateral South Asian regionalism rather than a tactical pause — with no realistic pathway for SAARC revival under current conditions.
Reason (R): The structural causes of SAARC's paralysis — the India-Pakistan conflict, the asymmetric power distribution, and the consensus-plus-exclusion charter rules — are unlikely to change in the foreseeable future, and India has concluded that investing political capital in SAARC revival produces lower returns than alternative platforms that exclude Pakistan's veto.
explanation: Both A and R are analytically defensible and causally linked. R identifies the specific structural conditions — enduring India-Pakistan conflict, asymmetric distribution, and unchangeable charter rules — that directly explain A's claim of pragmatic abandonment rather than tactical pause. India's consistent pattern since the 2016 SAARC cancellation — engaging BIMSTEC, pushing BBIN, prioritising bilaterals, and not pressing for summit revival — reflects a strategic calculation that the returns on alternative platforms exceed those from SAARC investment. While Indian officials maintain formal commitment to SAARC, the behavioural pattern supports A's reading of actual policy direction.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R identifies precisely the structural conditions and strategic calculation that A claims drive India's abandonment pattern — the two statements connect as explanation and conclusion rather than as parallel observations.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students see A's policy observation and R's strategic analysis as parallel when R is precisely the logic that explains A's outcome.

elimination_hints.hint: R's structural claims are well-documented — the India-Pakistan conflict has shown no signs of resolution, the asymmetric power distribution is geographic and unchangeable, and charter amendments would themselves require unanimity blocked by Pakistan.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume the structural conditions described in R might change when the veto dynamics actually lock them in place.

elimination_hints.hint: A is supported by India's actual behaviour since 2016 — no SAARC summits held, BIMSTEC summits hosted, Neighbourhood First bilateralism prioritised, no diplomatic push for SAARC revival despite rhetorical commitments.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students accept Indian officials' formal rhetorical commitment to SAARC as negating the observable behavioural abandonment.

assertion: The fact that SAARC has a larger combined population and economic weight than ASEAN yet has produced dramatically less meaningful regional integration demonstrates that size and resources alone do not determine regional integration success.
reason: Regional integration success depends primarily on political factors — the presence or absence of structural bilateral conflict, the pattern of power distribution (whether balanced or asymmetric), and the degree of shared external threat perception — rather than on demographic or resource endowment which can even become liabilities when combined with dysfunctional politics.
question_text:
Assertion (A): The fact that SAARC has a larger combined population and economic weight than ASEAN yet has produced dramatically less meaningful regional integration demonstrates that size and resources alone do not determine regional integration success.
Reason (R): Regional integration success depends primarily on political factors — the presence or absence of structural bilateral conflict, the pattern of power distribution (whether balanced or asymmetric), and the degree of shared external threat perception — rather than on demographic or resource endowment which can even become liabilities when combined with dysfunctional politics.
explanation: Both A and R are analytically sound and causally connected. R identifies the specific political variables — bilateral conflict, power distribution, shared threat perception — that explain why SAARC's larger resources have not translated into integration success as A claims. The comparison is widely used in regional integration literature (notably by scholars like Amitav Acharya) to demonstrate that political architecture trumps material endowment. ASEAN's more balanced power distribution, absence of structural bilateral paralysis, and shared concerns about both Cold War pressures and China's rise created favourable political conditions that SAARC has lacked, producing very different outcomes despite SAARC's larger material base.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R provides the causal mechanism — political factors dominating over material factors — that directly explains A's empirical observation about size failing to produce integration; these are explanation and observation, not parallel points.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students see A's empirical comparison and R's theoretical claim as independent observations when R specifically explains why A's pattern occurs.

elimination_hints.hint: R's claim about political primacy is the standard finding in regional integration literature — Amitav Acharya, Peter Katzenstein and others have demonstrated political factors dominate material ones in explaining outcomes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students question the theoretical primacy of political factors when this is well-established in regionalism scholarship.

elimination_hints.hint: A is factually supported — SAARC population (~1.9 billion) is far larger than ASEAN (~680 million), yet ASEAN has higher intra-regional trade, more institutions, and more effective cooperation than SAARC despite the size disadvantage.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students doubt A's factual comparison when the SAARC-larger but ASEAN-more-integrated pattern is a standard benchmark in regional integration studies.

question_text:
Arrange the following events in the correct chronological order showing the institutional evolution of SAARC:

(i) SAARC Secretariat established in Kathmandu
(ii) SAFTA Agreement signed, committing members to regional free trade area
(iii) Afghanistan admitted as the eighth SAARC member at the 14th Summit in New Delhi
(iv) 19th SAARC Summit in Islamabad cancelled after India and three other members withdraw following the Uri attack

items.text: SAFTA Agreement signed, committing members to regional free trade area
Afghanistan admitted as the eighth SAARC member at the 14th Summit in New Delhi
19th SAARC Summit in Islamabad cancelled after India and three other members withdraw following the Uri attack

explanation: The SAARC Secretariat was established in Kathmandu on January 16, 1987 (i). SAFTA was signed at the 12th SAARC Summit in Islamabad in January 2004 (ii). Afghanistan was admitted at the 14th Summit held in New Delhi in April 2007 (iii). The 19th Summit scheduled for November 2016 in Islamabad was cancelled after India withdrew following the September 2016 Uri attack (iv). This sequence shows SAARC's institutional construction (1987), economic deepening (2004), membership expansion (2007), and political paralysis (2016).
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: i → ii → iii → iv
ii → i → iii → iv
i → iii → ii → iv
ii → iii → i → iv

elimination_hints.hint: SAFTA (ii, 2004) came after the Secretariat was established (i, 1987) — the administrative body preceded the free trade agreement by 17 years, not the reverse.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place SAFTA before the Secretariat because SAFTA is more widely known than the Secretariat establishment date.

elimination_hints.hint: Afghanistan's admission (iii, 2007) came after SAFTA (ii, 2004) — SAFTA was signed three years before Afghanistan joined; this reverses the actual order.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the expansion of membership with the deepening of economic cooperation, placing membership expansion before SAFTA.

elimination_hints.hint: The Secretariat (i, 1987) came first — before both SAFTA (ii, 2004) and Afghanistan's admission (iii, 2007); this option completely inverts the early sequence.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students underestimate how early the basic SAARC institutional infrastructure was set up relative to substantive economic and membership developments.

question_text:
Arrange the following developments in the correct chronological sequence showing the emergence of alternative regional frameworks alongside SAARC's institutional decline:

(i) BIMSTEC formed in Bangkok with founding members including Bangladesh, India, Sri Lanka and Thailand
(ii) BBIN Motor Vehicles Agreement signed in Thimphu after Pakistan blocked the SAARC-wide version
(iii) India hosts BIMSTEC leaders alongside the BRICS Summit in Goa, symbolically pivoting away from SAARC
(iv) SAARC COVID-19 emergency video conference convened ad hoc by PM Modi — the first SAARC-format meeting in four years

items.text: BIMSTEC formed in Bangkok with founding members including Bangladesh, India, Sri Lanka and Thailand
BBIN Motor Vehicles Agreement signed in Thimphu after Pakistan blocked the SAARC-wide version
India hosts BIMSTEC leaders alongside the BRICS Summit in Goa, symbolically pivoting away from SAARC
SAARC COVID-19 emergency video conference convened ad hoc by PM Modi — the first SAARC-format meeting in four years

explanation: BIMSTEC was formed in Bangkok in June 1997 (i). The BBIN Motor Vehicles Agreement was signed in Thimphu in June 2015 (ii) after Pakistan blocked a SAARC-wide version at the 18th Kathmandu Summit in November 2014. India hosted BIMSTEC leaders alongside the BRICS Summit in Goa in October 2016 (iii) — just weeks after the 19th SAARC Summit cancellation, making the pivot explicit. The SAARC COVID-19 video conference was convened in March 2020 (iv) — four years after the Islamabad cancellation — notable because it was the first SAARC-format meeting since then, though still not a formal summit.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: i → ii → iii → iv
ii → i → iii → iv
i → iii → ii → iv
ii → iii → i → iv

elimination_hints.hint: BIMSTEC (i, 1997) was formed nearly two decades before BBIN (ii, 2015) — BIMSTEC emerged as an early alternative framework long before BBIN; the sequence cannot be reversed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume BBIN came first because it is more narrowly South Asian while BIMSTEC bridges South and Southeast Asia.

elimination_hints.hint: The Goa BIMSTEC-BRICS outreach (iii, October 2016) came after BBIN (ii, June 2015) — BBIN was already a year old when the Goa symbolic pivot occurred.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place the symbolic Goa pivot before practical sub-regional arrangements like BBIN.

elimination_hints.hint: BIMSTEC (i, 1997) was established well before BBIN (ii, 2015) — the 1997 founding far predates the 2015 motor vehicles agreement; this option inverts the foundational sequence.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students misdate BIMSTEC as a post-2014 innovation when it has existed since 1997 but gained prominence only later.

question_text: The SAARC Charter explicitly permits majority voting on economic and technical matters while requiring unanimity only on political questions, which is why SAFTA and other economic agreements have been able to move forward despite India-Pakistan political disputes.
explanation: This statement is false. The SAARC Charter (Article X) requires unanimity on all decisions without distinguishing between economic, technical, and political matters — there is no majority voting category in the Charter. SAFTA itself was negotiated and signed through the consensus route, not through majority voting, which is precisely why negotiating it took years and why its implementation has been repeatedly hampered by large sensitive lists, non-tariff barriers, and the India-Pakistan MFN deadlock. The false premise in the statement — that some SAARC matters are subject to majority voting — is a common misconception; in reality, the unanimity rule applies universally and is a key reason for the organisation's broad underperformance including SAFTA's limited impact (intra-SAARC trade remains only ~5%).
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: True
False

question_text:
Study the following comparative table showing key indicators of SAARC and ASEAN regional integration performance:

| Indicator | SAARC | ASEAN | What it reveals |
|-----------|-------|-------|-----------------|
| Founded | 1985 (Dhaka) | 1967 (Bangkok) | ASEAN has ~18 year head start |
| Members | 8 (including Afghanistan 2007) | 10 | Comparable sizes |
| Combined population | ~1.9 billion | ~680 million | SAARC nearly 3x larger |
| Intra-regional trade | ~5% of members' total trade | ~25% of members' total trade | ASEAN 5x more integrated |
| Summits held | 18 (last in 2014, Kathmandu) | Held regularly twice a year | SAARC effectively paralysed since 2016 |
| Free trade agreement | SAFTA 2006 — large sensitive lists, MFN deadlock | ATIGA — substantial tariff elimination | ASEAN's FTA is far more operational |
| Power distribution | India ~75% of regional GDP | No dominant member (Indonesia largest but not overwhelming) | SAARC has asymmetric hegemon, ASEAN more balanced |
| Structural bilateral conflict | India-Pakistan (nuclear, Kashmir, terrorism) | No comparable active dispute | Bilateral conflict paralyses SAARC but not ASEAN |
| External partnerships | Limited SAARC Observer status | ASEAN Plus Three, ASEAN Regional Forum, ASEAN Centrality | ASEAN has become a regional convening hub |

Based on the data in the table, which of the following conclusions is MOST accurately supported?
explanation: The table shows that SAARC's underperformance cannot be explained by temporal factors (18 years is not enough to explain a 5x trade integration gap), demographic factors (SAARC is nearly 3x larger population), or resource factors. The data instead highlights three political-structural variables where SAARC is disadvantaged: the presence of a dominant hegemon (India at ~75% of regional GDP), the presence of structural bilateral conflict (India-Pakistan), and the resulting institutional paralysis (no summits since 2014/2016 cancellation). ASEAN lacks all three of these disadvantages, which explains its relative success as a regional hub. This confirms the theoretical framework that regional integration depends on political architecture rather than material endowment.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: SAARC's underperformance is primarily explained by its younger age and smaller combined population compared to ASEAN — both factors that will correct naturally with time.
ASEAN performs better because it has abandoned consensus decision-making in favour of majority voting, while SAARC still uses outdated unanimity rules.

SAARC's underperformance reflects structural political disadvantages — asymmetric Indian hegemony, unresolved India-Pakistan conflict, and resulting institutional paralysis — which ASEAN lacks, confirming that political architecture matters more than demographic or material size in regional integration.
The table shows SAARC and ASEAN are performing at comparable levels across all meaningful indicators, and the perceived performance gap is an exaggeration in the literature.

elimination_hints.hint: The table shows SAARC is nearly 3x larger in population than ASEAN and only 18 years younger — neither can explain a 5x trade integration gap; age and size actually favour SAARC, not ASEAN.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students reach for demographic and temporal explanations when the table specifically shows those factors cut against SAARC underperformance rather than causing it.

elimination_hints.hint: ASEAN also operates on consensus (the 'ASEAN Way') — it has not abandoned consensus for majority voting; the advantage is political context (no paralysing bilateral conflict), not voting rules.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students misattribute ASEAN's success to institutional rules when both organisations use consensus; the difference is the political conditions in which consensus operates.

elimination_hints.hint: The table shows multiple dramatic gaps — 5% vs 25% intra-regional trade, 18 summits (paralysed since 2014) vs twice-yearly regular meetings, SAFTA sensitive-list-ridden vs ATIGA substantial — these are not comparable performance levels.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students underweight the table's quantitative gaps and dismiss the well-documented SAARC-ASEAN divergence as exaggeration.
